source_texts: Cosette stared at him in amazement, not uttering a word. Jean Valjean’s warning and the Thénardier woman’s name had petrified her.
The Prince produced the twenty-five-rouble note and held it out to Ferdyshchenko. He unfolded it, inspected it, then turned it over and brought it up to the light.
‘Tonight. All surveillance appears to have vanished from the Nautilus. There seems to be a total stupor on board. Will you be ready?’

He was not allowed to finish and persuaded to take his seat. The others implored him to calm down. Ganya, beside himself with rage, withdrew into a corner. Nina Alexandrovna was trembling all over and crying.

“I’ve been a widow for two years,” she said in a half whisper, shuddering as she spoke. “It was hard being married to him, he was old and beat me terribly. So when he lay sick, I looked at him and I said to myself: ‘If he gets better, he’ll get up and then what?’ And that is when the thought came to me . . .”
. . ." He wanted badly to get rid of me, however, and walked hurriedly across the street to the other sidewalk.

The famous doctor said his goodbyes with a serious expression, but one that hadn’t given up hope. And to the timid question Ivan Ilyich put to him, raising eyes that were shining with fear and hope—is there any possibility of recovery?—he answered that though one couldn’t guarantee it, there was a possibility. The look of hope with which Ivan Ilyich said goodbye to the doctor was so pitiful that, when she saw it, Praskovya Fyodorovna burst into tears as she went through the study doors to give the famous doctor his fee.

“Ah!” he exclaimed, interrupting himself. “There come Mignon and Steiner! They are always together. You know that Steiner begins to have had enough of Rose; so the husband sticks to him like a plaster lest he should escape.”

“It was all thanks to Kamsa that I was able to enter into this city,” said Vandiya Devan and started recounting yet another adventure of his. Vandiya Devan knew that the city would be on alert and guards would be posted at the gates to capture him. They also knew that he would come bearing the Pazhuvur Signet ring so he couldn’t possibly use it to gain entry.
‘How can you doubt it! But you, who do not love her, why are you so ready to endanger your life? It must be that you hate Erik.’
rationales: Outputs 2 and 3 share multiple distinctive stylistic features: both use 'stunned silence,' both use 'frozen' to describe Cosette's state, and both use the nearly identical phrase 'mention of the Thénardier woman's name.' They also restructure the original similarly, separating Cosette's reaction from its cause in a two-sentence format. These parallel choices strongly suggest the same paraphraser.

Outputs 0 and 4 share nearly identical structure and vocabulary: both use 'a twenty-five-rouble note,' 'unfolded the bill,' 'flipped it over,' and 'held it up to the light.' The second sentence in both follows the same four-action list format. Minor differences (e.g., 'handed' vs. 'offered,' 'carefully inspected' vs. 'examined') are small variations typical of the same paraphrasing system.

Outputs 1 and 2 share the same stylistic fingerprints: no quotation marks, use of 'Tonight,' the identical phrase 'in a complete daze,' and ending with 'Are you prepared?' Both adopt a similarly casual but clean register, differing only slightly in sentence structure (1 combines into one sentence, 2 splits into two). These shared word choices and tonal patterns strongly suggest the same paraphraser.

Outputs 0 and 4 share the same distinctive opening phrase 'They did not let him finish and sat him down again,' the same ending 'Nina Alexandrovna trembled and wept' (word-for-word identical), and a similarly literal, close-to-source translation style. Both also use 'Ganya went off to/in a corner in a fury' with minimal restructuring, contrasting sharply with outputs 1, 2, and 3 which all use passive constructions and more significant rephrasing.

Outputs 1 and 2 share the most consistent stylistic fingerprints: both use 'two years,' both describe the husband as treating her 'brutally,' both use a trembling/murmuring speech attribution, both modernize the language with similar phrasing ('difficult' marriage, conditional recovery phrasing), and both end with an ellipsis. Their vocabulary choices and sentence restructuring approach are nearly identical, suggesting the same paraphrasing system.

Outputs 1 and 4 are essentially identical: 'He was eager to be rid of me and quickly crossed the street to the opposite sidewalk.' The only difference is a leading ellipsis in output 1, which is a minor formatting artifact. The exact same word choices, sentence structure, and phrasing ('eager to be rid of me,' 'quickly crossed the street,' 'opposite sidewalk') indicate they were produced by the same paraphrasing system.

Outputs 2 and 4 share nearly identical phrasing and structure: both use 'renowned physician,' 'grave yet not entirely hopeless look on his face,' 'nervously asked,' 'heartbreaking,' 'Praskovya Fyodorovna,' 'distinguished physician his fee,' and similar constructions like 'while nothing was/could be certain, a chance did exist/the possibility existed.' Their sentence-level structure is closely mirrored throughout, indicating the same paraphraser.

Outputs 0 and 4 share the distinctive word 'inseparable' (not used by any other output), both use 'Ah!' as the exclamation, both phrase it as the husband staying close to prevent Steiner from slipping/getting away, and both have very similar register and sentence structure. These linguistic fingerprints strongly suggest they came from the same paraphraser.

Outputs 0 and 3 share the most consistent stylistic fingerprint. Both use nearly identical three-sentence structures, capitalize 'Pazhuvur Signet ring' the same way, use 'on high alert' with 'guards stationed at' the gates/entrances 'to capture/catch him,' and conclude with the same logical explanation about not being able to use the signet ring. Their sentence lengths, vocabulary choices, and overall flow are the most closely matched among all outputs.

Outputs 1 and 2 are nearly identical in structure and phrasing: both begin with 'How could you possibly question [that/it]!', both use 'But you, who have no [feelings/love] for her, what makes you so willing to risk your own life?', and both end with 'You must truly despise Erik.' The only differences are single word substitutions ('that' vs 'it' and 'feelings' vs 'love'), which strongly indicates they were produced by the same paraphraser with slight variation.

“Tonight. It seems all surveillance has disappeared from the Nautilus. There appears to be a complete stupor on board. Will you be prepared?”
They did not let him finish and sat him down again; they began begging him to calm himself. Ganya, in fury, went to the far corner. Nina Alexandrovna trembled and wept.

"I am a widow these three years," she began in a half-whisper, with a sort of shudder. "I had a hard life with my husband. He was an old man. He used to beat me cruelly. He lay ill; I thought looking at him, if he were to get well, if he were to get up again, what then? And then the thought came to me--"
But the man wanted absolutely to get rid of me, and he sheered off, in all haste, to the other side of the street.

The renowned doctor took his leave with a grave expression, though not one entirely without hope. And when Ivan Ilyich, lifting eyes bright with fear and hope, timidly asked whether there was any chance of recovery, he replied that while nothing could be promised, there was still a possibility. The hopeful look with which Ivan Ilyich parted from the doctor was so heartbreaking that, upon seeing it, Praskovya Fyodorovna broke into tears as she passed through the study doors to pay the famous doctor his fee.
